title: Rare Events: Imbalance, Fraud & Default
tag: 2% positives breaks the defaults — know the toolkit
why: Default and fraud are 1-3% events, and rarity breaks the standard recipe: accuracy becomes meaningless, the 0.5 threshold is absurd, and the loss is dominated by the majority class so the model barely learns the minority. This toolkit exists because the naive pipeline silently produces a model that looks excellent and catches nothing.
intuition: Default and fraud are 0.5–3% events, and rarity breaks the standard recipe three ways: accuracy becomes meaningless (chapter 11), the 0.5 threshold becomes absurd, and the loss is dominated by the majority class so the model barely learns the minority. Every fintech loop probes this; have the toolkit ordered in your head.
visualization: Picture two histograms on the same axis: a tall mountain of good loans and, dwarfed beside it, a tiny hill of defaults — 50 to 1. The 0.5 threshold sits far out in the tail of the big mountain, so almost everything it flags is a false alarm. Now picture sliding the threshold and watching precision and recall trade off — that sliding is exactly the PR curve in the calib·PR tab, and the no-skill line sits at the prevalence, not 0.5.
mathematics:
P(fraud | flag) = P(flag | fraud)·P(fraud) ⁄ P(flag)
“With a 2% base rate, even a 90%-sensitive flag is mostly false alarms — precision is governed by the prior.” Restating Bayes the moment imbalance comes up is the reflex interviewers are listening for. Then add the operational consequence: alert-queue capacity, not model quality, often sets the threshold.
code:
# Lab
Taiwan credit, artificially starved
1. Downsample positives to ~3% to simulate a real book
2. Fit naive vs class_weight='balanced'; compare PR-AUC and recall at fixed 5% alert rate
3. Plot both models' predicted-PD histograms — see the weighted one shift right
4. Recalibrate the weighted model (CalibratedClassifierCV) and re-check the calibration curve
failure: Every fix has a cost. Class weights and resampling SHIFT the predicted probabilities, so the model is no longer calibrated and its outputs cannot be used as PDs without repair. SMOTE interpolates synthetic minority points that can blur the true class boundary and, if applied before the train/test split, leaks across folds. And evaluating on a resampled set flatters you — the true performance lives on the original, untouched distribution.

interview.s30: “With a 2% base rate, even a 90%-sensitive flag is mostly false alarms — precision is governed by the prior.

sharpen: You balanced the classes and recall shot up. Why might your predicted probabilities now be unusable for pricing, and what repairs them?
A colleague applies SMOTE to the full dataset, then splits into train and test. What did they just leak?
With 2% positives, what does a Bayes base-rate calculation tell you about the precision of even a 90%-sensitive flag?

blocks.t: Default and fraud are 0.5–3% events, and rarity breaks the standard recipe three ways: accuracy becomes meaningless (chapter 11), the 0.5 threshold becomes absurd, and the loss is dominated by the majority class so the model barely learns the minority. Every fintech loop probes this; have the toolkit ordered in your head.
The toolkit, in order of preference

First: do nothing to the data — use class weights (class_weight='balanced', or scale_pos_weight in XGBoost), which reweight the loss so minority errors cost more. Second: move the THRESHOLD, not the data — pick it from the cost matrix. Third, if you must resample: random undersampling of the majority is simple and honest; SMOTE interpolates synthetic minority points — mention it, then voice the skepticism that it can blur class boundaries and leak across CV folds if applied before splitting. Resampling order matters: split first, resample inside the training fold only.

“With a 2% base rate, even a 90%-sensitive flag is mostly false alarms — precision is governed by the prior.” Restating Bayes the moment imbalance comes up is the reflex interviewers are listening for. Then add the operational consequence: alert-queue capacity, not model quality, often sets the threshold.

items: Reweighting or resampling SHIFTS predicted probabilities — recalibrate before using them as PDs
Evaluate on the TRUE, untouched distribution — never on the resampled one
Use PR-AUC alongside ROC-AUC when positives are rare
Temporal validation is mandatory: random splits leak the future, and fraud patterns drift adversarially

Plot both models' predicted-PD histograms — see the weighted one shift right

title: Statistics for Experimentation
tag: A/B tests, p-values, power — the stats round
why: When a change shows a 1.2% lift, you need to know whether that's real or noise — otherwise you ship randomness and call it progress. Hypothesis testing, power, and the bootstrap are the discipline for separating signal from sampling variation, and top-tech loops test it even for applied scientists because shipping noise is expensive at scale.
intuition: Top-tech loops (Meta, Google, Stripe) include an experimentation round even for applied scientists: can you decide, under sampling noise, whether a change actually did anything? The machinery: assume a null hypothesis (no effect), compute how surprising your data would be if the null were true, and reject only when it is surprising enough.
visualization: Picture the bell curve of a sample mean (see the gauss tab): run an A/B test 1000 times under no real effect and the measured differences pile into a Gaussian centered at zero. Your one observed difference is a single dart on that distribution. The p-value is the shaded tail area beyond your dart — how far out you landed if nothing is really happening.
mathematics:
z = (p̂₁ − p̂₂) ⁄ SE ,    p-value = P(data this extreme | H₀)
“The z-statistic measures the observed difference in units of its own sampling noise.” And the sentence that catches half of all candidates: “a p-value is the probability of data this extreme GIVEN the null — it is NOT the probability the null is true.” Deliver that distinction cleanly and the round tilts your way.
code:
# Lab
numpy simulation + LendingClub
1. Simulate an A/B: two binomials, n = 5000 each, rates 4.0% vs 4.6%; compute z and the p-value
2. Wrap it in a loop of 1000 simulated experiments; measure power empirically, then re-run at n = 20000
3. Simulate peeking: check significance every 500 samples, stop at first hit — measure the inflated false-positive rate under a TRUE null
4. Bootstrap a 95% CI for your Taiwan model's Gini: resample rows 1000 times
failure: The framework is widely misused: a p-value is P(data | null), not P(null | data), and treating it as the latter is the most common error in the field. Peeking (checking repeatedly and stopping at significance) silently inflates false positives. At huge n, everything is 'significant' including effects too small to matter — statistical significance diverges from practical significance. And the tests assume the randomization actually held.

interview.s30: “The z-statistic measures the observed difference in units of its own sampling noise.

sharpen: Define a p-value precisely. Now state the wrong definition most people give — and why the difference matters.
Your A/B dashboard updates live and you check it hourly, shipping when it goes green. What's wrong with that?
At n = 10 million per arm, a 0.01% lift is significant. Do you ship it? What question matters more than the p-value here?

blocks.t: “The z-statistic measures the observed difference in units of its own sampling noise.” And the sentence that catches half of all candidates: “a p-value is the probability of data this extreme GIVEN the null — it is NOT the probability the null is true.” Deliver that distinction cleanly and the round tilts your way.
Power, peeking, and the bootstrap

Power = the probability of detecting an effect of a given size if it is real; it drives sample-size planning before launch — underpowered tests mostly produce noise. Peeking — checking repeatedly and stopping the moment significance appears — silently inflates the false-positive rate; fixed horizons or sequential corrections fix it. And when no formula exists for your statistic's uncertainty (a KS stat, a Gini), the bootstrap does: resample your sample with replacement, recompute the statistic each time, read the spread. It is the Σ-as-a-loop idea applied to inference.

Statistical ≠ practical significance: at n = 10M, a 0.01% lift is significant and worthless
Randomization is what licenses the causal claim — without it you have correlation with extra steps
For default-rate comparisons, the two-proportion z-test is your default tool; know its SE formula

title: Modern Deep Learning Blocks
tag: residuals, multi-head, positional encoding — the transformer, fully
why: Single-head attention works but leaves capability on the table, and attention alone is blind to word order. Multi-head attention, positional encoding, and pre-norm residual stacks are the refinements that turned the raw attention idea into a model that trains deep and reads sequences correctly. They're the most common deep-learning depth probes precisely because they separate users from understanders.
intuition: Chapter 9 gave you embeddings, LayerNorm, and FiLM. This chapter completes the transformer: why residual connections let depth work, why one attention head is not enough, and why a transformer is blind to word order without help. These three are the most common deep-learning depth probes in AS/RS loops.
mathematics:
y = x + F(x)
“Each block learns a CORRECTION to the identity, not a whole new mapping.” The why, in one breath: the derivative of the identity path is exactly 1, so during backprop the gradient flows through the + untouched no matter how deep the stack — depth stops killing gradients (chapter 6's vanishing problem, solved structurally). Without residuals, 100-layer networks simply do not train. This is the single most important architectural idea after attention itself.

MHA = W_O · concat(head₁ … head_h)
“Run h attention heads in parallel, each in its own learned subspace, concatenate, then mix.” The interview gold is the WHY: different heads learn different relation types, so the model captures multiple kinds of dependency at once — something a single head provably cannot.
code:
# Lab
pure numpy / a small PyTorch block
1. Build single-head attention; then split d=64 into 8 heads and verify output shape is unchanged after concat
2. Permute the input tokens; confirm outputs just permute (order-blindness) — then add sinusoidal PE and confirm they don't
3. Add a residual + LayerNorm around the block; check gradient magnitude at the input with vs without the skip
4. Inspect per-head attention maps on a toy sentence — look for different patterns
failure: Multi-head specialization is emergent, not guaranteed — heads can collapse to redundancy, and many are prunable with no loss, meaning the capacity is often wasted. Positional encoding schemes each have limits: fixed sinusoids extrapolate poorly beyond trained lengths, learned positions don't extrapolate at all, and even rotary (RoPE) degrades far past its training context. Residual stacks assume normalization is placed correctly; post-norm without warmup destabilizes.

interview.s30: “Each block learns a CORRECTION to the identity, not a whole new mapping.

sharpen: Heads specialize emergently. So why can you often prune half of them with almost no accuracy loss?
Shuffle the input tokens to a transformer with no positional encoding. What happens to the output, and why is that fatal for language?
Pre-norm vs post-norm: which trains deep stacks more stably without learning-rate warmup, and what's the mechanism?

blocks.t: Chapter 9 gave you embeddings, LayerNorm, and FiLM. This chapter completes the transformer: why residual connections let depth work, why one attention head is not enough, and why a transformer is blind to word order without help. These three are the most common deep-learning depth probes in AS/RS loops.
Residual connections: gradient highways

“Each block learns a CORRECTION to the identity, not a whole new mapping.” The why, in one breath: the derivative of the identity path is exactly 1, so during backprop the gradient flows through the + untouched no matter how deep the stack — depth stops killing gradients (chapter 6's vanishing problem, solved structurally). Without residuals, 100-layer networks simply do not train. This is the single most important architectural idea after attention itself.

One detail interviewers reward: WHERE the LayerNorm sits. The original transformer was post-norm (norm AFTER the residual add: x → F → add → norm), which needs careful learning-rate warmup to stay stable. Modern stacks are pre-norm (norm BEFORE the sublayer: x → norm → F → add), which keeps a clean identity path all the way through and trains deep models far more stably — it's why GPT-style models can stack dozens of layers without delicate warmup. Knowing pre- vs post-norm is a precise depth signal.
Multi-head: parallel subspaces

Single-head attention forces every relationship through one d-dimensional bottleneck. Multi-head splits the embedding into h subspaces, runs attention independently in each (its own learned Q/K/V projections), then concatenates and mixes with a final W_O. The payoff is emergent specialization: trained heads end up tracking different relations — one for adjacent syntax, one for subject-verb, one for long-range coreference — without ever being told to. Compute is roughly unchanged because each head works in a d/h space.

“Run h attention heads in parallel, each in its own learned subspace, concatenate, then mix.” The interview gold is the WHY: different heads learn different relation types, so the model captures multiple kinds of dependency at once — something a single head provably cannot.
Positional encoding: restoring order

Attention is permutation-invariant: shuffle the input tokens and the attention math gives the same set of outputs, because it is a weighted average with no notion of position. That is a disaster for language and time series, where order is meaning. The fix: ADD a position signal to each token embedding. The original transformer used fixed sinusoids of varying frequency (so relative offsets are linearly recoverable); modern models often learn positions or use rotary embeddings (RoPE) that rotate Q and K by an angle proportional to position. Know that the problem exists and the two families of fix — that depth of answer is usually enough.

Pre-norm (norm before sublayer) trains deep stacks stably; post-norm (original) needs LR warmup — say which and why
Heads specialize emergently; that is the entire argument for multi-head over single-head
Attention without positional encoding is order-blind — a permutation invariance you must be able to state
Sinusoidal (fixed) vs learned vs rotary (RoPE) — the three position schemes; RoPE dominates current LLMs

title: Self-Supervision & Foundation Models
tag: mandatory for 2026 loops · BERT → RLHF → LoRA → RAG → agents
why: Labelling data is the bottleneck — it's slow and expensive, and most data is unlabelled. Self-supervision breaks the bottleneck by manufacturing labels from the data itself (mask a token, predict it), enabling pretraining on oceans of text. The pretrain-then-adapt paradigm that follows (finetune, LoRA, RAG, RLHF) is the defining shift of modern ML and assumed knowledge in 2026 loops.
intuition: The defining shift of the last five years: instead of training a model per task on labelled data, you PRETRAIN one large model on oceans of unlabelled data via self-supervision, then ADAPT it cheaply. Every modern AS/RS loop assumes you can speak this stack fluently. Here is the whole pipeline, compressed.
visualization: Picture pretraining as filling a giant reservoir from rain (oceans of unlabelled text), and finetuning as running a thin pipe from that reservoir to one field (your task). Self-supervision is the trick that makes the rain usable: mask a word and predict it, so the data labels itself. LoRA is a small adjustable valve bolted onto the frozen pipe — you tune the valve (≈1% of parameters), never the reservoir.
mathematics:
pretrain (broad) → finetune (task) → RLHF (align)
“Pretraining learns general representations from unlabelled data; finetuning adapts them to a task with a little labelled data; RLHF aligns behaviour to human preferences via a learned reward model and policy optimization.” RLHF in one more sentence: collect human rankings of outputs, train a reward model on them, then optimize the LLM against that reward (PPO or, increasingly, DPO which skips the separate RL step).
code:
# Lab
HuggingFace, a small model, 30 minutes
1. Load a pretrained BERT; mask a token in a credit-policy sentence and read its top-5 fills
2. Finetune a tiny classifier head on Taiwan-credit text fields (or any small labelled set)
3. Attach a LoRA adapter (peft library); count trainable vs total params — see the <1%
4. Sketch a RAG loop: embed a doc store, retrieve top-k for a query, stuff into the prompt
failure: Foundation models inherit and amplify the biases in their pretraining data, and they hallucinate confidently — RAG mitigates but doesn't eliminate this, and adds a dependency on retrieval quality. Finetuning risks catastrophic forgetting; LoRA reduces but doesn't remove it. RLHF optimizes a learned reward model that can be gamed (reward hacking). And the whole stack is opaque — explaining a single decision to a regulator is far harder than with a scorecard.

interview.s30: “Pretraining learns general representations from unlabelled data; finetuning adapts them to a task with a little labelled data; RLHF aligns behaviour to human preferences via a learned reward model and policy optimization.

sharpen: LoRA trains under 1% of the parameters yet recovers most of full-finetune quality. What empirical fact about weight updates makes that possible?
RAG is supposed to reduce hallucination. Describe a case where it makes things worse.
Your FiLM-on-frozen-encoder approach IS parameter-efficient adaptation. Make that connection explicit to a foundation-models interviewer.

blocks.t: The defining shift of the last five years: instead of training a model per task on labelled data, you PRETRAIN one large model on oceans of unlabelled data via self-supervision, then ADAPT it cheaply. Every modern AS/RS loop assumes you can speak this stack fluently. Here is the whole pipeline, compressed.
Self-supervision: labels from the data itself

Self-supervised learning manufactures a supervised task out of unlabelled data. BERT's masked language modelling hides ~15% of tokens and trains the model to fill them — forcing bidirectional context understanding. GPT-style causal LM predicts the next token. Contrastive methods like SimCLR take two augmented views of the same image, and train embeddings so matching views attract and mismatched ones repel (an InfoNCE loss — cross-entropy over similarities, chapter 8 again). The unifying idea: the data supervises itself, so you can use billions of examples.
Pretrain → finetune → align

“Pretraining learns general representations from unlabelled data; finetuning adapts them to a task with a little labelled data; RLHF aligns behaviour to human preferences via a learned reward model and policy optimization.” RLHF in one more sentence: collect human rankings of outputs, train a reward model on them, then optimize the LLM against that reward (PPO or, increasingly, DPO which skips the separate RL step).
Efficient adaptation & retrieval

items: LoRA: freeze the giant model, inject small low-rank ΔW = BA adapters — train <1% of params, near-full-finetune quality. The 'why': weight updates are empirically low-rank
RAG: don't bake knowledge into weights — RETRIEVE relevant documents at inference and condition generation on them. Fixes staleness and hallucination, adds a retrieval quality dependency
Quantization (8/4-bit) + LoRA = QLoRA: finetune a 70B model on one GPU
Agents: an LLM in a loop with tools (search, code, APIs) and memory — plan, act, observe, repeat. The frontier and the failure-mode minefield

title: How to Read & Critique Papers
tag: the Research Scientist differentiator · problem → idea → tradeoff → next
why: Applied scientists use methods; research scientists interrogate why a method exists and what it cost. The difference shows in interviews as whether you can place a paper in its lineage rather than just explain its mechanism. This is a trainable reading protocol — the skill that separates someone who can critique from someone who can only implement.
intuition: Applied Scientists USE methods; Research Scientists interrogate WHY a method exists and what it cost. The interview tell is whether you can place a paper in its lineage — what problem it solved, what it broke, what came next — rather than just explaining its mechanism. This is a trainable reading protocol, not innate talent.
visualization: Picture a paper not as a slab of text but as one arrow in a chain: PROBLEM → existing fix → its limit → NEW IDEA → tradeoff → what broke → next paper. Attention is one arrow (RNNs were sequential and forgetful → let every token see every token → but O(n²)). Reading well means locating the single arrow this paper adds, then tracing backward and forward along the chain.
mathematics:
“What problem was attention solving that RNNs couldn't?”
Answer in lineage form: “RNNs are sequential, so they're slow to train and lose long-range signal through a fixed hidden state. Attention lets every position see every other in parallel — solving both — at the cost of quadratic complexity, which became the NEXT problem the field attacked.” That structure — problem, idea, tradeoff, next — is the Research Scientist answer. Mechanism alone is the Applied Scientist answer.
code:
# Lab
the Attention paper + one paper from your domain
1. Read ONLY the abstract and intro; write the 6-link lineage chain before reading further
2. Find the main ablation table; for each row, name what it isolates
3. Write three reviewer critiques you'd raise if assigned this paper
4. Do all three for PRAGMA — out loud, as if defending it
failure: The lineage template can mislead if you take the paper's own framing at face value — authors motivate their problem to flatter their solution, and a weakened baseline can make a modest idea look revolutionary. Reading abstract-first risks anchoring on the claim before you've checked the evidence. And the template assumes a clean problem→solution arc; some influential work is messier (an empirical surprise nobody predicted) and doesn't fit the chain.

title: Experimental Design & Rigor
tag: the most important missing skill · 'how do you know it wasn't noise?'
why: A method is only as good as the evidence that it works. As models and datasets grew, it became easy to report improvements that were really noise, leakage, or a lucky split. Experimental rigor exists to separate a real finding from an artifact — it's the discipline that makes a result trustworthy, and it's weighted above method knowledge in research loops for exactly that reason.
intuition: The question that ends weak candidates: “your model improved AUC by 1.2% — how do you know it wasn't noise?” Research and senior applied loops weight experimental rigor above method knowledge, because a clever method evaluated sloppily is worthless. This chapter is the discipline that separates a result from an artifact.
mathematics:
“Is 1.2% within run-to-run variance?”
The full answer: “First I'd check whether 1.2% exceeds the seed-to-seed standard deviation — retrain both models over multiple seeds and compare the distributions, not point estimates. Then a paired test on per-fold or per-example scores, since the models saw the same data. For AUC specifically, DeLong's test gives a paired significance test on the SAME cases, and a bootstrap gives a CI on the difference. Then confirm the gain holds out-of-time, not just on a lucky random split.” Seeds, DeLong/bootstrap, out-of-time — name those and the round is yours.
code:
# Lab
any two models on Taiwan credit
1. Retrain both over 10 seeds; report mean ± std AUC — is your 'winner' inside the other's band?
2. Run DeLong's test (or a bootstrap) on the paired AUC difference for significance
3. Ablate one component of the better model; attribute how much of the gain it owns
4. Slice AUC by one demographic/segment column; check the gain isn't one group carrying it
failure: Rigor itself doesn't fail, but its absence does, predictably: a single-seed result reported as a win, an ablation that changes two things at once so attribution is impossible, a random split that leaks the future, or an evaluation tuned after peeking at the test set. The subtle one is distribution shift — concept shift in particular silently invalidates a model whose inputs still look normal, so even a clean experiment can mislead if train and deployment distributions diverge.

interview.s30: Before believing an improvement I check it isn't noise: multiple seeds with mean and standard deviation, a paired test like DeLong on the same cases for AUC, and confirmation that the gain holds out-of-time, not just on a lucky split. Then ablations to attribute the cause.
interview.m2: The instinct is to never trust a single point estimate. Retrain over seeds and compare distributions; for AUC specifically DeLong gives a paired significance test and a bootstrap gives a CI on the difference. Ablate one component at a time to attribute the gain — was it the conditioning or just the parameter count, the architecture or just better-tuned hyperparameters. Then error analysis: slice the metric by segment because an average can hide a per-group regression, and inspect the worst predictions for label noise. Finally guard against distribution shift — covariate (P(x)), label (P(y)), and concept (P(y|x) breaks) — which is what invalidated delinquency models in 2020.
interview.m5: Center the whole answer on 'how do you know it wasn't noise', and answer in layers: variance across seeds, paired significance (DeLong/bootstrap), out-of-time validation. Then ablations as the attribution tool, with the discipline of changing exactly one thing and tuning baselines as hard as the method. Move into error analysis and slicing — the failure structure is more informative than the headline number — and name the three shifts precisely, with the 2020 forbearance example for concept shift. Bring in your unfair advantage: model validation under SR 11-7 is adversarial experimental design — independent replication, sensitivity analysis, challenger benchmarking — so you've done rigorous evaluation for a living. That reframing lands harder than any technique because almost no candidate has shipped under a regulator.

sharpen: Your model improved AUC by 1.2%. Walk me through exactly how you'd determine whether that's real.
Your FiLM model beats the baseline. Design the ablations that would convince a skeptic it was the conditioning and not just the extra parameters.
Train and test both come from 2019. The model deploys in 2020. Which kind of distribution shift worries you most and why?
An average AUC gain hides a regression on one customer segment. How would your evaluation have caught it, and what do you report?

blocks.t: The question that ends weak candidates: “your model improved AUC by 1.2% — how do you know it wasn't noise?” Research and senior applied loops weight experimental rigor above method knowledge, because a clever method evaluated sloppily is worthless. This chapter is the discipline that separates a result from an artifact.

The full answer: “First I'd check whether 1.2% exceeds the seed-to-seed standard deviation — retrain both models over multiple seeds and compare the distributions, not point estimates. Then a paired test on per-fold or per-example scores, since the models saw the same data. For AUC specifically, DeLong's test gives a paired significance test on the SAME cases, and a bootstrap gives a CI on the difference. Then confirm the gain holds out-of-time, not just on a lucky random split.” Seeds, DeLong/bootstrap, out-of-time — name those and the round is yours.
Ablations: isolate the cause

An ablation removes ONE component at a time to attribute the gain. If your FiLM model beats the baseline, the ablations must rule out the alternative explanations: was it the conditioning, or just the extra parameters? (ablate FiLM but keep the param count). Was it the architecture, or better hyperparameters? (tune the baseline equally hard). A result without ablations is a correlation, not a finding — interviewers probe exactly here.
Error analysis & the rest of the toolkit

items: Error analysis: don't report one number — look at WHERE the model fails (which segments, which slices). The failure structure is usually more informative than the headline metric
Counterfactual / slice analysis: does the gain come from one subpopulation? Average improvement can hide per-group regression

Three kinds of shift, name them precisely: COVARIATE shift = inputs P(x) move (caught by PSI); LABEL shift = class proportions P(y) move; CONCEPT shift = the relationship P(y|x) itself breaks — e.g. 2020 forbearance severed the usual delinquency→default link and invalidated the models
OOD detection: flag inputs far from training data via density, embedding distance, or predictive uncertainty — don't score what you've never seen
Reproducibility: fixed seeds, logged hyperparameters, versioned data — a result you can't reproduce isn't one
Dataset bias: is the benchmark representative, or does it embed the very pattern you're 'discovering'?

blocks.t: Your regulatory background is a hidden strength here: model validation under SR 11-7 IS adversarial experimental design — independent replication, sensitivity analysis, benchmarking against challengers. You have done rigorous evaluation for a living; name it that way.

steps: Retrain both over 10 seeds; report mean ± std AUC — is your 'winner' inside the other's band?
Run DeLong's test (or a bootstrap) on the paired AUC difference for significance
Ablate one component of the better model; attribute how much of the gain it owns
Slice AUC by one demographic/segment column; check the gain isn't one group carrying it

caseStudy.title: COVID-19 model failures (concept shift)
caseStudy.body: In early 2020, credit and fraud models across the industry degraded suddenly — not because inputs drifted (covariate shift) but because the relationship itself broke (concept shift). Forbearance programs meant a borrower could be 90+ days past due by the old definition while not actually defaulting, severing the delinquency→default link every PD model relied on. Models that monitored only input PSI saw nothing wrong; the failure was invisible until labels matured months later. It's the canonical lesson that a clean offline experiment cannot protect you from a world that changes the mapping P(y|x).

title: Learning Theory (Intuition Only)
tag: for research roles · VC, Rademacher, SRM — pictures not proofs
why: Why should low training error imply low test error at all? Without an answer, ML is alchemy. Learning theory provides the intuition: generalization is possible only if your model class isn't flexible enough to fit any labelling by accident. You don't need the proofs, but you need to know why capacity and data size trade off — it's the rigor under regularization and bias-variance.
intuition: Research loops occasionally probe whether you understand WHY generalization is even possible. You don't need the proofs; you need the pictures. The central question: why should low training error imply low test error at all? Learning theory's answer: only if your model class isn't so flexible that it can fit any labelling by accident.
visualization: Picture the generalization gap as a spring: model capacity (VC dimension) stretches train and test error apart, while training data n pulls them back together. test ≲ train + √(capacity/n). A 2D line shatters 3 points but not 4 — that ‘4’ is its capacity made concrete. Overparameterized nets stretch the spring enormously yet still generalize, which is why the classical picture is incomplete.
mathematics:
test_err ≲ train_err + √( capacity ⁄ n )
“Generalization error is bounded by training error plus a complexity penalty that grows with model capacity and shrinks with more data.” That single inequality IS the intuition behind every learning bound. You don't need to derive it — you need to read it: capacity up, gap up; data up, gap down.
code:
# Lab
thought experiment + sklearn
1. Take 3 points in 2D; enumerate all 8 labellings and confirm a line separates each (shatters)
2. Add a 4th point in XOR position; find the labelling no line can fit (VC = 3 confirmed)
3. Train a high-capacity model on RANDOM labels; watch it reach ~0 train error — that's high Rademacher complexity made visible
4. Connect to ch. 10: relate the complexity penalty to the λ in ridge
failure: Classical bounds (VC, Rademacher) are famously loose — for deep nets they're often vacuous, predicting terrible generalization for models that generalize fine, because capacity counted naively is enormous. The theory assumes i.i.d. data from a fixed distribution, which deployment violates. So the honest position is that these bounds explain why simple models generalize and motivate regularization, but don't yet explain modern deep learning.

interview.s30: “Generalization error is bounded by training error plus a complexity penalty that grows with model capacity and shrinks with more data.

sharpen: A 2D linear classifier shatters 3 points but not 4. What does that '3' actually measure, and why does it cap generalization gap?
Overparameterized nets have huge VC dimension yet generalize well. What does that tell you about the classical bounds?
How is the complexity penalty in a generalization bound related to the λ in ridge regression — and to Structural Risk Minimization?

blocks.t: Research loops occasionally probe whether you understand WHY generalization is even possible. You don't need the proofs; you need the pictures. The central question: why should low training error imply low test error at all? Learning theory's answer: only if your model class isn't so flexible that it can fit any labelling by accident.
VC dimension: capacity as a number

The VC dimension of a model class is the largest number of points it can 'shatter' — label in all possible ways. A 2D linear classifier shatters 3 points but not 4 (VC = 3). Intuition: higher VC = more flexible = more capacity to memorize = a bigger gap between training and test error possible. Generalization bounds say roughly: test error ≤ train error + (something growing with VC, shrinking with n). More capacity needs more data to pin down.

“Generalization error is bounded by training error plus a complexity penalty that grows with model capacity and shrinks with more data.” That single inequality IS the intuition behind every learning bound. You don't need to derive it — you need to read it: capacity up, gap up; data up, gap down.
Rademacher & SRM

items: Rademacher complexity: a tighter, data-dependent capacity measure — can your class fit RANDOM noise labels on YOUR data? If yes, it can overfit. More practical than VC because it uses the actual data distribution

Structural Risk Minimization: don't just minimize training error — minimize training error PLUS a complexity penalty. This is literally what regularization (ch. 10) does; SRM is its theoretical justification
The deep-learning puzzle: huge networks have enormous VC dimension yet generalize — classical bounds are loose here; implicit regularization from SGD is part of the modern answer
Bias-variance (ch. 10) is the practical shadow of these bounds — same trade, different language

blocks.t: If pushed in an interview, the honest senior answer is: “Classical VC bounds explain why simple models generalize and motivate regularization via SRM, but they're vacuous for overparameterized deep nets — that gap between theory and practice is an active research area.” Knowing the limits of the theory signals more depth than reciting it.

title: Causal Inference & Time Series
tag: AS staples · uplift, DiD, IV · ARIMA, Kalman, TFT
why: Prediction asks 'who will default?'; decisions ask 'what happens if we INTERVENE — raise the limit, send the offer?' Correlation-based models break under intervention because they learned associations, not effects. Causal inference is the toolkit for estimating effects honestly, and time series is the native shape of the macro and mortgage data you forecast — both recur constantly in applied loops.
intuition: Two domains that recur in applied loops and map directly onto your work. Causal inference answers 'what is the EFFECT of an action', not just 'what correlates' — essential whenever a model informs a decision (offer this loan? raise this limit?). Time series is the native shape of macro and mortgage data.
visualization: Picture two parallel worlds for one borrower: the one where you raised their limit and the one where you did not. You only ever see one — the other is the missing counterfactual, the gray ghost. All of causal inference is machinery to estimate that ghost: randomization makes the two groups interchangeable; difference-in-differences lines up two trend lines and reads the gap that opens after treatment.
mathematics:
ATE = E[Y | do(T=1)] − E[Y | do(T=0)]
“The average treatment effect compares outcomes in the world where everyone is treated vs no one — the do-operator means intervene, not observe.” The fundamental problem: you never see both worlds for the same unit. Randomization (A/B testing) solves it by making treatment independent of confounders. When you can't randomize, you approximate that condition.
code:
# Lab
FRED macro data + LendingClub
1. Fit ARIMA to a macro series (unemployment, HPI); inspect residual autocorrelation
2. Build a propensity model on Taiwan credit; weight to balance a pseudo-treatment, compare naive vs adjusted effect
3. Sketch a DiD: pick a policy date, plot treated vs control trends around it
4. Frame one PRAGMA scenario as a conditional forecast — connect FiLM to TFT-style conditioning out loud
failure: Every observational causal method rests on an untestable assumption: propensity scoring assumes no unobserved confounders, difference-in-differences assumes parallel trends, instruments assume the exclusion restriction. If the assumption fails, the estimate is confidently wrong with no warning from the data itself. Time series adds its own trap — non-stationarity breaks most classical models, and shuffling time in cross-validation leaks the future.

interview.s30: “The average treatment effect compares outcomes in the world where everyone is treated vs no one — the do-operator means intervene, not observe.

sharpen: You can't observe both the treated and untreated outcome for the same borrower. So how does randomization rescue you, and what does it actually guarantee?
Difference-in-differences gives a clean effect estimate. What single assumption is it betting everything on, and how would you check it?
Why is random k-fold cross-validation a mistake for a default-forecasting time series, and what do you do instead?

Causal inference: effects, not correlations

“The average treatment effect compares outcomes in the world where everyone is treated vs no one — the do-operator means intervene, not observe.” The fundamental problem: you never see both worlds for the same unit. Randomization (A/B testing) solves it by making treatment independent of confounders. When you can't randomize, you approximate that condition.

items: Propensity scores: model P(treated | covariates), then match or weight so treated and control look comparable — fakes randomization from observational data
Difference-in-Differences: compare the CHANGE in treated vs control around an intervention, cancelling fixed differences. Needs the parallel-trends assumption — state it
Instrumental Variables: when an unobserved confounder lurks, find an instrument that moves treatment but not the outcome directly (e.g. distance-to-branch for credit access)
Uplift modeling: predict the INCREMENTAL effect of an action per customer (who defaults ONLY IF we raise the limit) — not P(default), but ΔP(default | action). The credit-decision frontier

blocks.t: Time series: the macro/mortgage shape

Classical: ARIMA models a series as autoregression (depends on its own past) plus moving-average (depends on past shocks), after differencing to remove trend — the baseline every forecaster must know. State-space / Kalman filters track a hidden state evolving over time, optimally fusing a noisy prediction with each new noisy observation (Bayesian updating in motion — your priors meeting data). Prophet is the pragmatic trend+seasonality+holidays decomposition.

items: Deep: LSTMs for sequence forecasting; the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) blends attention with gating for multi-horizon forecasts WITH interpretable variable importance — directly relevant to macro-conditional risk
Stationarity is the core assumption — difference or detrend first; a non-stationary series breaks most classical models
Temporal cross-validation only: never shuffle time (chapter 23's leakage lesson)
Your CCAR stress paths ARE conditional time-series scenarios — TFT and FiLM-on-sequences is exactly your research neighborhood

title: Credit Risk ML: PD · LGD · EAD · IFRS9 · CCAR
tag: your home turf, interview-packaged
why: Regulators require banks to quantify and reserve against credit loss, and to do it under stress and forward-looking economic conditions. The whole apparatus — PD/LGD/EAD, IFRS9/CECL, CCAR, Basel — exists to translate 'how much could we lose?' into capital and provisions. This is your home turf; the chapter packages it so you can make it legible to interviewers who don't live in it.
intuition: This is your domain — the chapter exists so you can PACKAGE it for interviewers who don't live in it, and so the fintech loops (DBS, Emirates NBD, digital banks) hear a candidate who owns the regulatory stack cold. The expected loss identity is the spine everything hangs from.
visualization: Picture expected loss as a funnel with three gates in series: PD (will it default?) narrows the flow, LGD (how much is lost if it does?) narrows it again, EAD (how much is exposed?) scales it. EL = PD × LGD × EAD is the trickle out the bottom. Capital is not this trickle but the rare flood — a high quantile in the tail of the same loss distribution.
mathematics:
EL = PD × LGD × EAD
“Expected loss factors into probability of default, loss given default, and exposure at default — three models, multiplied.” Then place each: PD is a classification problem (your logistic/GBM, chapters 4–12); LGD is a bounded regression, often a two-stage cure-vs-loss model; EAD predicts the drawn balance at default. Saying which ML problem each factor is shows you see the modelling under the regulation.
code:
# Lab
Freddie Mac (you have the parser)
1. Build the three EL components separately; multiply to a portfolio EL
2. Recompute EL under a stressed HPI/unemployment path — quantify the CCAR delta
3. Contrast 12-month vs lifetime EL on the same book (IFRS9 staging intuition)
4. Write the 3-sentence pitch connecting EL, IFRS9, and FiLM for a risk-modeling interviewer
failure: The EL = PD × LGD × EAD decomposition assumes the three components are independent — but in a downturn they correlate viciously (defaults rise exactly when collateral values fall, so PD and LGD spike together), which is why downturn-LGD and stressed scenarios exist. Point-in-time PD is responsive but procyclical; through-the-cycle is stable but slow to react. And every model here is only as good as its macro scenario, which is assumed, not observed.

interview.s30: “Expected loss factors into probability of default, loss given default, and exposure at default — three models, multiplied.

sharpen: EL = PD × LGD × EAD treats the three as independent. Why does that assumption fail precisely when it matters most?
IFRS9 wants point-in-time, forward-looking PD; Basel leans through-the-cycle. What's the tension, and why can't one number serve both?
Frame your FiLM research as solving a specific regulatory need. What's the one-sentence pitch connecting EL, IFRS9, and conditioning?

The regulatory map

items: IFRS 9 / CECL: forward-looking EXPECTED credit loss provisioning. IFRS 9 uses 12-month vs lifetime EL by stage; CECL (US) is lifetime from day one. Both demand macro-conditional, point-in-time PD — exactly what PRAGMA addresses
CCAR / stress testing: project losses under supervisor-defined adverse macro scenarios. Your dynamic stress-regime work lives here
Basel: regulatory CAPITAL via risk-weighted assets; capital covers UNexpected loss (a tail quantile, ch. 3), provisions cover expected loss — know which is which
Through-the-cycle (stable, Basel) vs point-in-time (responsive, IFRS9) PD — the philosophical split you can discuss fluently

blocks.t: Why your PRAGMA story lands here

Frame it as: regulators now require PD that responds to macro scenarios (IFRS9 forward-looking, CCAR stress), but bolting macro onto a static model means retraining per scenario. FiLM conditioning on a frozen encoder gives scenario-responsive PD without retraining — regulatory need meets modern architecture. That sentence connects your day job, your research, and the role in one breath.

title: ML System Design: Credit & Fraud
tag: the design round — graded on structure, not brilliance
why: A model is 5% of a production credit or fraud system; the other 95% — labels, features, serving, monitoring, governance — is what design rounds actually test. The round exists because shipping a model that works in a notebook but fails in production (stale features, leaked labels, undetected drift) is the most expensive mistake an applied scientist can make.
intuition: The round: 45 minutes, “design a real-time fraud detection system” or “design the PD model lifecycle for a digital bank.” You drive; the interviewer steers. They are grading whether you cover the lifecycle in order with explicit trade-offs — not whether you name an exotic architecture. Memorize a skeleton and hang everything on it.
mathematics:
the 30-second opener
“Before modeling, I want to pin down three things: what decision this score drives and the cost asymmetry, how the label is defined and when it matures, and the latency and regulatory constraints. Then I'll walk data, model, evaluation, deployment, and monitoring.” Open every design question with that paragraph and you are in the top decile before touching a model.
code:
# Lab
a 25-minute timer and a blank page
1. Prompt: “design the PD modeling platform for a digital bank in Singapore”
2. Write the seven steps with two concrete decisions each — no prose, just the skeleton fleshed
3. Speak it aloud once against the timer
4. Bring it to me for a graded mock with follow-up probes
failure: Designs fail by skipping the unglamorous parts: defining the label wrong (no maturity window, so immature accounts bias the rate), training/serving skew (features computed differently online vs offline), and monitoring only performance (but labels arrive months late, so drift must be caught on inputs first). The clever-architecture answer that ignores the lifecycle is the one that doesn't get the offer.

interview.s30: “Before modeling, I want to pin down three things: what decision this score drives and the cost asymmetry, how the label is defined and when it matures, and the latency and regulatory constraints.

sharpen: What's the FIRST thing you clarify before touching a model in a fraud-detection design question — and why is five minutes there never wasted?
Labels for default arrive months after origination. What does that force you to monitor on day one of deployment?
Where in your design does your SR 11-7 model-validation experience become an advantage no other candidate has?

blocks.t: The round: 45 minutes, “design a real-time fraud detection system” or “design the PD model lifecycle for a digital bank.” You drive; the interviewer steers. They are grading whether you cover the lifecycle in order with explicit trade-offs — not whether you name an exotic architecture. Memorize a skeleton and hang everything on it.
The seven-step skeleton

items: 1 · Clarify: business objective, costs of FP vs FN, latency budget, regulatory constraints — five minutes here is never wasted
2 · Label: the hardest part nobody expects — fraud confirms via chargebacks weeks later; default = 90+DPD within a 12-month window; immature accounts must be excluded (label maturity)
3 · Data & features: entity aggregates, velocity features, bureau pulls — all computed POINT-IN-TIME; using future information is the classic leakage
4 · Model: GBM as the stated baseline, deep/sequential models where structure demands it — and say WHY, citing the tabular-vs-sequence trade
5 · Offline eval: temporal (out-of-time) split, AUC/KS + calibration + a cost curve at the operating threshold
6 · Deployment: feature store for online-offline consistency, shadow mode, champion–challenger before cutover
7 · Monitoring: PSI on inputs and scores (labels arrive late, so drift shows up there first), performance tracking once labels mature, retrain triggers

title: The Interview Playbook
tag: saying math out loud, under pressure
why: Interviews don't test whether you can read math silently — they test whether you can narrate it under pressure. The gap between knowing a concept and saying it crisply in 30 seconds is real and trainable. This chapter exists because strong candidates lose offers on delivery, not knowledge, and rehearsed verbalization closes that gap.
intuition: Interviews don't test whether you can read equations silently — they test whether you can narrate them. Five rules: (1) name the output first; (2) read role-by-role, never symbol-by-symbol; (3) state ranges — “maps the reals to (0,1)” is high-signal; (4) flag bookkeeping explicitly — “the front factor just normalizes”; (5) after any formula, volunteer the one-line intuition unprompted. Rule 5 is where offers are made.
code:
# Lab
a friend, or me, plus ISLR chapter 4
1. Have them pick any display equation at random
2. 60-second timer: run the four passes out loud, ending with the plain-English sentence and the Python one-liner
3. They ask one “what happens if…” follow-up (e.g., what if η doubles? what if T → 0?)
4. Two clean rounds per day; log which symbol classes still slow you down
failure: Rehearsed answers fail when they're recited rather than understood — an interviewer's one follow-up ('what if η doubles?') exposes a memorized script instantly. Over-preparing canned lines also makes you rigid: you pattern-match the question to your script instead of answering what was actually asked. The fix isn't more scripts, it's understanding deep enough that the 30-second answer is a compression, not a recitation.
sharpen: Pick any formula from this lab. Give the 30-second answer, then have someone ask one 'what happens if...' follow-up. Did you bend or break?
Which of the trap questions in this chapter would you still get wrong under pressure — and why is recognizing that more useful than re-reading the answer?
Explain log-loss to a smart non-ML colleague in three sentences. If you can't, do you actually understand it?

blocks.t: Interviews don't test whether you can read equations silently — they test whether you can narrate them. Five rules: (1) name the output first; (2) read role-by-role, never symbol-by-symbol; (3) state ranges — “maps the reals to (0,1)” is high-signal; (4) flag bookkeeping explicitly — “the front factor just normalizes”; (5) after any formula, volunteer the one-line intuition unprompted. Rule 5 is where offers are made.
The greatest hits — rehearse these verbatim

items: σ(z): “squashes any real number into a probability; it's the inverse of the log-odds”
log-odds = βᵀx: “a unit of feature j multiplies the odds by e^βj”
log-loss: “pay minus-log of the probability you gave the actual outcome; 0.693 is the coin-flip baseline”
θ ← θ − η∇L: “an update rule, not an equation; minus because gradients point uphill”
softmax: “exponentiate and normalize; only gaps between logits matter”
attention: “score every pair, scale by √d to keep softmax healthy, weighted-average the values”
multi-head: “h heads in parallel subspaces; specialization is emergent — that's why it beats single-head”
residual x+F(x): “identity path has derivative 1, so gradients survive depth”
boosting: “each tree fits the negative gradient of the loss — descent in function space”
AUC: “P(random positive outranks random negative); Gini = 2·AUC−1”
LoRA: “freeze the model, train tiny low-rank adapters — updates are empirically low-rank”
KL: “expected extra surprise from believing Q when reality is P; PSI is its symmetrized cousin”
Bayes: “posterior = likelihood × prior / evidence — and always check the base rate”
1.2% AUC gain: “is it inside seed-to-seed variance? paired test, out-of-time, then I believe it”

blocks.t: The daily drill (15 minutes)

Ten questions in the quiz tab — any topic where the streak breaks, reopen that chapter and its interactive tab. One formula narrated aloud while walking, full interview voice. One lab cell re-typed from memory, no peeking. Equation fluency is a motor skill: it is built by speaking and typing, not by re-reading.

blocks.data: a friend, or me, plus ISLR chapter 4
steps: Have them pick any display equation at random
60-second timer: run the four passes out loud, ending with the plain-English sentence and the Python one-liner
They ask one “what happens if…” follow-up (e.g., what if η doubles? what if T → 0?)
Two clean rounds per day; log which symbol classes still slow you down

keyFacts: σ(z): “squashes any real number into a probability; it's the inverse of the log-odds”
log-odds = βᵀx: “a unit of feature j multiplies the odds by e^βj”
log-loss: “pay minus-log of the probability you gave the actual outcome; 0.693 is the coin-flip baseline”
θ ← θ − η∇L: “an update rule, not an equation; minus because gradients point uphill”
softmax: “exponentiate and normalize; only gaps between logits matter”
attention: “score every pair, scale by √d to keep softmax healthy, weighted-average the values”
multi-head: “h heads in parallel subspaces; specialization is emergent — that's why it beats single-head”
residual x+F(x): “identity path has derivative 1, so gradients survive depth”
boosting: “each tree fits the negative gradient of the loss — descent in function space”
AUC: “P(random positive outranks random negative); Gini = 2·AUC−1”
LoRA: “freeze the model, train tiny low-rank adapters — updates are empirically low-rank”
KL: “expected extra surprise from believing Q when reality is P; PSI is its symmetrized cousin”
Bayes: “posterior = likelihood × prior / evidence — and always check the base rate”
